name: Heibai Wuchang
description: Heibai Wuchang, literally translated as "Black and White Impermanence," are a pair of deities in Chinese folk religion and mythology who serve as assistants to Yanluo Wang, the judge of the underworld. They are responsible for escorting the spirits of the deceased to the underworld for judgment. Hei Wuchang (Black Impermanence) and Bai Wuchang (White Impermanence) are often depicted as contrasting figures, with Hei Wuchang typically wearing a tall black hat inscribed with the Chinese characters for "Seeing Him, Prosperity," and Bai Wuchang wearing a tall white hat inscribed with "Seeing Him, Misfortune." Their contrasting appearances and inscriptions reflect the duality of their role: bringing either prosperity to those who respect the dead or misfortune to those who do not. They are revered and feared, embodying the cyclical nature of life and death, and the importance of proper funerary rites and ancestor veneration. Their images are commonly found in temples and during funeral processions, serving as reminders of the inevitable journey to the afterlife. They are also associated with ghosts and spirits, mediating between the living and the dead.
subtitle: Black and White Impermanence

keyMyths.title: The Story of Xie Bi'an and Fan Wujiu
keyMyths.description: One popular myth recounts that Heibai Wuchang were originally two friends, Xie Bi'an (Bai Wuchang) and Fan Wujiu (Hei Wuchang), who were sworn brothers. They agreed to meet under a bridge, but when Fan Wujiu arrived early, he was swept away by a flood while waiting. Xie Bi'an, true to his word, waited for his brother and eventually drowned himself in grief. Touched by their loyalty, the gods appointed them as deities of the underworld. This myth explains their close association and their roles in escorting souls.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E. T. C. *Myths and Legends of China*. George G. Harrap & Co., 1922.

keyMyths.title: Escorting Souls to the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Heibai Wuchang are primarily responsible for locating and escorting the souls of the recently deceased to the underworld. Hei Wuchang is often depicted as capturing the souls of those who have died prematurely or unexpectedly, while Bai Wuchang is said to guide the souls of those who have lived out their natural lives. They work together to ensure that all souls are accounted for and brought before Yanluo Wang for judgment.
keyMyths.source: Teiser, Stephen. *The Scripture on the Ten Kings*. University of Hawaii Press, 1994.

keyMyths.title: Punishment and Reward
keyMyths.description: While their primary function is to escort souls, Heibai Wuchang are also associated with the administration of justice in the underworld. They are said to oversee the punishments and rewards meted out to souls based on their actions in life. Their contrasting appearances symbolize the duality of this process, with Hei Wuchang representing the harsh punishments and Bai Wuchang representing the potential for redemption and reward.
keyMyths.source: Dean, Kenneth. *Taoist Ritual and Popular Cults of Southeast China*. Princeton University Press, 1993.

sources.author: Werner, E. T. C.
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends, providing context for the role of Heibai Wuchang in the Chinese pantheon.

sources.work: The Scripture on the Ten Kings
sources.description: A study of the Ten Kings tradition in Chinese Buddhism, which is relevant to understanding the structure of the underworld and the role of deities like Heibai Wuchang.
